en.title: TypeScript Literal Types
en.description: Literal Types in TypeScript restrict a variable to specific values, enhancing code precision and catching errors during development.

content.text: Literal Types in TypeScript allow a variable to hold only a specific value. For example, `let status: "success"` restricts the variable to only the value `"success"`. Literal types can be strings, numbers, or booleans and are often used with union types, like `"success" | "error"`, meaning the variable can only be one of those values. Literal types improve code precision and safety, as TypeScript detects incorrect values during development.

Literal Types ensure a variable is restricted to a specific value or a set of values. For example, `type Direction = "up" | "down"` allows a variable to only be `"up"` or `"down"`. They can be used in functions, objects, or with type narrowing. Type narrowing (using `if` or `switch`) helps manage literal types effectively. When combined with union types, literal types provide flexibility and precision, but only allow the specified values.

Literal types are very useful in real projects. For example, in an online store, order statuses like `"pending"`, `"shipped"`, or `"delivered"` can be defined using literal types. In games, movement directions (`"up"`, `"down"`, `"left"`, `"right"`) or button states (`true`, `false`) benefit from literal types for precision. They‚Äôre also used to restrict API response statuses or configuration values. Below is a simple store example.

Literal types apply TypeScript‚Äôs static typing to enforce specific values, preventing errors common in JavaScript where any value can be assigned. They ensure variables only take predefined values, improving code precision and safety. In projects at companies like Microsoft, Google, and Airbnb, literal types enhance code quality, especially for statuses, configurations, or restricted values.

items.description: Restrict variables to specific string values, e.g., `"success" | "error"`.
Restrict variables to specific numbers, e.g., `1 | 2 | 3`.
Restrict variables to `true` or `false`.
Literal types are often combined with union types, e.g., `"up" | "down"`.
Function parameters or return values can be literal types.
Object properties can be restricted with literal types.
Use `switch` or `if` to handle literal types precisely.
Smart suggestions and error warnings for literal types in tools like VSCode.

content.text:
- **Dangers**: Using too many literal values can make code complex. Incorrect type narrowing may lead to runtime errors. Overusing literal types can reduce code flexibility.
- **Recommendations**: Use literal types only when specific values are needed, apply type narrowing correctly, avoid `any`, choose simple and clear values, and use IDEs like VSCode to catch errors early.

- **–ě–Ņ–į—Ā–Ĺ–ĺ—Ā—ā–ł**: –ė—Ā–Ņ–ĺ–Ľ—Ć–∑–ĺ–≤–į–Ĺ–ł–Ķ —Ā–Ľ–ł—ą–ļ–ĺ–ľ –Ī–ĺ–Ľ—Ć—ą–ĺ–≥–ĺ –ļ–ĺ–Ľ–ł—á–Ķ—Ā—ā–≤–į –Ľ–ł—ā–Ķ—Ä–į–Ľ—Ć–Ĺ—č—Ö –∑–Ĺ–į—á–Ķ–Ĺ–ł–Ļ –ľ–ĺ–∂–Ķ—ā —É—Ā–Ľ–ĺ–∂–Ĺ–ł—ā—Ć –ļ–ĺ–ī. –Ě–Ķ–Ņ—Ä–į–≤–ł–Ľ—Ć–Ĺ–ĺ–Ķ —Ā—É–∂–Ķ–Ĺ–ł–Ķ —ā–ł–Ņ–ĺ–≤ –ľ–ĺ–∂–Ķ—ā –Ņ—Ä–ł–≤–Ķ—Ā—ā–ł –ļ –ĺ—ą–ł–Ī–ļ–į–ľ –≤–ĺ –≤—Ä–Ķ–ľ—Ź –≤—č–Ņ–ĺ–Ľ–Ĺ–Ķ–Ĺ–ł—Ź. –ß—Ä–Ķ–∑–ľ–Ķ—Ä–Ĺ–ĺ–Ķ –ł—Ā–Ņ–ĺ–Ľ—Ć–∑–ĺ–≤–į–Ĺ–ł–Ķ literal types –ľ–ĺ–∂–Ķ—ā —Ā–Ĺ–ł–∑–ł—ā—Ć –≥–ł–Ī–ļ–ĺ—Ā—ā—Ć –ļ–ĺ–ī–į.
- **–†–Ķ–ļ–ĺ–ľ–Ķ–Ĺ–ī–į—Ü–ł–ł**: –ė—Ā–Ņ–ĺ–Ľ—Ć–∑—É–Ļ—ā–Ķ literal types —ā–ĺ–Ľ—Ć–ļ–ĺ –ī–Ľ—Ź –Ĺ–Ķ–ĺ–Ī—Ö–ĺ–ī–ł–ľ—č—Ö –ļ–ĺ–Ĺ–ļ—Ä–Ķ—ā–Ĺ—č—Ö –∑–Ĺ–į—á–Ķ–Ĺ–ł–Ļ, –Ņ—Ä–į–≤–ł–Ľ—Ć–Ĺ–ĺ –Ņ—Ä–ł–ľ–Ķ–Ĺ—Ź–Ļ—ā–Ķ —Ā—É–∂–Ķ–Ĺ–ł–Ķ —ā–ł–Ņ–ĺ–≤, –ł–∑–Ī–Ķ–≥–į–Ļ—ā–Ķ `any`, –≤—č–Ī–ł—Ä–į–Ļ—ā–Ķ –Ņ—Ä–ĺ—Ā—ā—č–Ķ –ł –Ņ–ĺ–Ĺ—Ź—ā–Ĺ—č–Ķ –∑–Ĺ–į—á–Ķ–Ĺ–ł—Ź, –ł—Ā–Ņ–ĺ–Ľ—Ć–∑—É–Ļ—ā–Ķ IDE, —ā–į–ļ–ł–Ķ –ļ–į–ļ VSCode, –ī–Ľ—Ź —Ä–į–Ĺ–Ĺ–Ķ–≥–ĺ –ĺ–Ī–Ĺ–į—Ä—É–∂–Ķ–Ĺ–ł—Ź –ĺ—ą–ł–Ī–ĺ–ļ.
